title_en: Hadiths 31–42: The Final Hadiths — Renunciation, Intention, and the Universality of Mercy
content_en:
The thirty-first hadith of al-Arba'een an-Nawawiyyah is one of the most beloved in the entire collection: "Be in this world as though you were a stranger or a traveler passing through" (Sahih al-Bukhari). Ibn Umar, who narrated this hadith, is said to have added: "When evening comes, do not expect to live until morning; and when morning comes, do not expect to live until evening." Al-Nawawi explains that the essence of zuhd (renunciation of worldly attachment) is not poverty or rejection of permissible comforts, but rather not allowing material concerns to occupy the heart at the expense of the Hereafter.

The thirty-second hadith reinforces the concept of zuhd through the Prophet's prohibition of harming others: "There should be neither harm nor reciprocation of harm." This hadith, though brief, is foundational in Islamic jurisprudence — scholars have derived entire fields of fiqh (Islamic law) regarding property rights, medical ethics, environmental protection, and social welfare from it. Al-Nawawi treats it as one of the most comprehensive legal principles in the Sunnah.

Hadiths thirty-three and thirty-four address the concept of truth-telling and the prohibition of litigation without right. The Prophet ﷺ warned against taking oaths falsely and against claiming property that does not belong to one. Al-Nawawi notes that dishonesty in commercial and legal transactions destroys trust, which is the foundation of both personal relationships and social order in Islam.

The thirty-fifth hadith is another comprehensive principle: "Do not be envious of one another, do not inflate prices against one another, do not hate one another, do not turn away from one another, do not undercut one another in trade — and be servants of Allah, brothers" (Sahih Muslim). Al-Nawawi identifies envy, price manipulation, hatred, and boycotting as the primary social vices that destroy community cohesion. The positive complement — brotherhood in Allah — is the antidote to each of these vices.

The thirty-sixth hadith contains the well-known principle: "Whoever relieves a believer of one of the hardships of this world, Allah will relieve him of one of the hardships of the Day of Resurrection." Al-Nawawi explains that this hadith establishes a principle of divine reciprocity — the more one serves others, the more Allah's mercy attends to one's own needs. This motivates the believer to engage in charitable and social work not merely out of altruism but as an act of worship.

The final hadiths (37–42) of al-Arba'een address topics of intention, following the Prophet's example, the obligation of seeking knowledge, and the comprehensive nature of Islamic ethics. The collection concludes with the hadith: "Allah has written down the good deeds and the evil deeds. Then He clarified that — whoever intends a good deed but does not do it, Allah writes it as a full good deed for him; and if he intends and performs it, Allah writes it as ten good deeds up to seven hundred times or many times over." Al-Nawawi ends his commentary with gratitude to Allah for facilitating the completion of this work, which has served as a cornerstone of Islamic education for centuries.
content_id:
Hadits ketiga puluh satu dari al-Arba'een an-Nawawiyyah adalah salah satu yang paling dicintai dalam seluruh kumpulan ini: "Jadilah di dunia ini seakan-akan engkau adalah seorang asing atau musafir yang sedang melintas" (Shahih al-Bukhari). Ibnu Umar, yang meriwayatkan hadits ini, dikatakan telah menambahkan: "Apabila tiba waktu sore, janganlah engkau mengharap hidup hingga pagi; dan apabila tiba waktu pagi, janganlah engkau mengharap hidup hingga sore." Al-Nawawi menjelaskan bahwa hakikat zuhd (melepaskan diri dari keterikatan duniawi) bukanlah kemiskinan atau penolakan terhadap kenyamanan yang diperbolehkan, melainkan tidak membiarkan urusan materi menguasai hati hingga mengorbankan kepentingan Akhirat.

Hadits ketiga puluh dua memperkuat konsep zuhd melalui larangan Nabi ﷺ untuk menyakiti orang lain: "Tidak boleh ada bahaya dan tidak boleh ada tindakan saling membahayakan." Hadits ini, meskipun singkat, merupakan landasan dalam fikih Islam — para ulama telah menurunkan seluruh bidang fikih (hukum Islam) mengenai hak kepemilikan, etika medis, perlindungan lingkungan, dan kesejahteraan sosial darinya. Al-Nawawi memperlakukannya sebagai salah satu kaidah hukum paling komprehensif dalam Sunnah.

Hadits ketiga puluh tiga dan ketiga puluh empat membahas konsep kejujuran dan larangan berperkara tanpa hak. Nabi ﷺ memperingatkan tentang pengambilan sumpah secara palsu dan tentang mengklaim harta yang bukan miliknya. Al-Nawawi mencatat bahwa ketidakjujuran dalam transaksi komersial dan hukum merusak kepercayaan, yang merupakan fondasi hubungan antarpribadi maupun tatanan sosial dalam Islam.

Hadits ketiga puluh lima adalah kaidah komprehensif lainnya: "Janganlah kalian saling mendengki, janganlah kalian saling menaikkan harga, janganlah kalian saling membenci, janganlah kalian saling berpaling, dan janganlah sebagian kalian menjual di atas jualan sebagian yang lain — jadilah hamba-hamba Allah yang bersaudara" (Shahih Muslim). Al-Nawawi mengidentifikasi dengki, manipulasi harga, kebencian, dan pemboikotan sebagai keburukan sosial utama yang merusak kohesi komunitas. Pelengkap yang bersifat positif — persaudaraan karena Allah — adalah penawar bagi setiap keburukan tersebut.

Hadits ketiga puluh enam mengandung kaidah yang dikenal luas: "Barangsiapa melepaskan dari seorang mukmin satu kesulitan dari kesulitan-kesulitan dunia, Allah akan melepaskan darinya satu kesulitan dari kesulitan-kesulitan Hari Kiamat." Al-Nawawi menjelaskan bahwa hadits ini menetapkan prinsip timbal balik ilahi — semakin seseorang melayani orang lain, semakin rahmat Allah menyertai kebutuhannya sendiri. Hal ini memotivasi seorang mukmin untuk terlibat dalam amal dan kegiatan sosial bukan sekadar karena altruisme, melainkan sebagai sebuah ibadah.

Hadits-hadits terakhir (37–42) dari al-Arba'een membahas topik niat, mengikuti teladan Nabi ﷺ, kewajiban menuntut ilmu, dan sifat etika Islam yang menyeluruh. Kumpulan ini diakhiri dengan hadits: "Allah telah menetapkan amal-amal kebaikan dan amal-amal keburukan. Kemudian Dia menjelaskannya — barangsiapa berniat melakukan suatu kebaikan namun tidak melaksanakannya, Allah menuliskannya baginya sebagai satu kebaikan penuh; dan jika ia berniat lalu melaksanakannya, Allah menuliskannya sebagai sepuluh kebaikan hingga tujuh ratus kali lipat atau bahkan berlipat ganda." Al-Nawawi mengakhiri komentarnya dengan rasa syukur kepada Allah karena telah memudahkan selesainya karya ini, yang telah menjadi landasan pendidikan Islam selama berabad-abad.
title_id: Hadits 31–42: Hadits-Hadits Terakhir — Zuhud, Niat, dan Universalitas Rahmat
content_ar: The thirty-أولاً الحديث of al-Arba'een an-Nawawiyyah is one of the most beloved in the entire collection: "Be in this الدنيا as though you were a stranger or a traveler passing through" (صحيح البخاري). Ibn عمر, who narrated this الحديث, is said to have added: "عندما evening comes, do not expect to live حتى morning; and عندما morning comes, do not expect to live حتى evening." النووي explains that the essence of الزهد (renunciation of worldly attachment) is not الفقر or rejection of permissible comforts, but rather not allowing material concerns to occupy the القلب at the expense of الآخرة. The thirty-ثانياً الحديث reinforces the concept of الزهد through النبي ﷺ's prohibition of harming others: "There should be neither harm nor reciprocation of harm." This الحديث, though brief, is foundational in Islamic jurisprudence — العلماء have derived entire fields of الفقه (Islamic law) فيما يخص property الحقوق, medical ethics, environmental protection, and social welfare from it. النووي treats it as one of the most comprehensive legal principles in the السنة. Hadiths thirty-three and thirty-four address the concept of الحق-telling and the prohibition of litigation without الحق. النبي ﷺ warned against taking oaths falsely and against claiming property that does not belong to one. النووي notes that dishonesty in commercial and legal transactions destroys trust, which is the foundation of both personal relationships and social order in الإسلام. The thirty-خامساً الحديث is another comprehensive principle: "Do not be envious of بعضهم بعضاً, do not inflate prices against بعضهم بعضاً, do not الكراهية بعضهم بعضاً, do not turn away from بعضهم بعضاً, do not undercut بعضهم بعضاً in trade — and be servants of الله, brothers" (صحيح مسلم). النووي identifies الحسد, price manipulation, hatred, and boycotting as the الأساسي social vices that destroy الجماعة cohesion. The positive complement — brotherhood in الله — is the antidote to each of these vices. The thirty-سادساً الحديث contains the well-known principle: "Whoever relieves a المؤمن of one of the hardships of this الدنيا, الله will relieve him of one of the hardships of the Day of البعث." النووي explains that this الحديث establishes a principle of divine reciprocity — the more one serves others, the more الله's الرحمة attends to one's own needs. This motivates the المؤمن to engage in charitable and social work not merely out of altruism but as an act of worship. The final hadiths (37–42) of al-Arba'een address topics of intention, following النبي ﷺ's example, the الالتزام of seeking العلم, and the comprehensive nature of Islamic ethics. The collection concludes with the الحديث: "الله has written down the الخير deeds and the الشر deeds. Then بيَّن that — whoever intends a الخير deed but does not do it, الله writes it as a full الخير deed for him; and إذا he intends and performs it, الله writes it as ten الخير deeds up to
